perturbation.original_text: If Ingredients provided by Supplier under this Agreement are misbranded, contaminated, or otherwise unfit for human consumption at the time they are delivered to PNC or its TPM ("Defect"), PNC in its sole discretion will make a determination of the necessity of a recall, market withdrawal, inventory retrieval, or other action designed to prevent the distribution or sale of the affected Finished Products, plus the type, extent, method of handling, disposition of the Finished Products as well as any affected work in progress, and all other particulars involved in such an action (a "Recall"), and PNC will execute any Recall. Supplier, in its sole discretion, will make a determination of the necessity of a recall, market withdrawal, inventory retrieval or other action designed to prevent the distribution or sale of the Ingredients.
perturbation.changed_text: If Ingredients provided by Supplier under this Agreement are misbranded, contaminated, or otherwise unfit for human consumption at the time they are delivered to PNC or its TPM ("Defect"), PNC in its sole discretion will make a determination of the necessity of a recall, market withdrawal, inventory retrieval, or other action designed to prevent the distribution or sale of the affected Finished Products, plus the type, extent, method of handling, disposition of the Finished Products as well as any affected work in progress, and all other particulars involved in such an action (a "Recall"), and PNC may execute any Recall, if it deems necessary. Supplier, in its sole discretion, is the final authority on whether a recall is necessary, market withdrawal, inventory retrieval or other action designed to prevent the distribution or sale of the Ingredients should take place.
perturbation.explanation: By changing the language to give the Supplier 'final authority' over recalls, we create ambiguity and potentially contradict regulations that place the onus of food safety and recall decisions on manufacturers or distributors in certain scenarios. This conflicts with the idea of shared responsibility for food safety.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 21 U.S. Code § 350l - Authority to request records
perturbation.law_explanation: 21 U.S. Code § 350l grants the FDA authority to request records related to food safety, indicating a federal interest in ensuring recalls are appropriately initiated. By granting the supplier 'final authority,' this contract language could be interpreted as an attempt to limit or impede the ability of the manufacturer (PNC) to execute a recall deemed necessary by regulators or even dictated by the FDA, thus conflicting with the intent and potentially the requirements of food safety laws.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: U.S.C. Title 21 - FOOD AND DRUGS 21 U.S.C. United States Code, 2011 Edition Title 21 - FOOD AND DRUGS CHAPTER 9 - FEDERAL FOOD, DRUG, AND COSMETIC ACT SUBCHAPTER IV - FOOD Sec. 350l - Mandatory recall authority From the U.S. Government Publishing Office, www.gpo.gov §350 l . Mandatory recall authority (a) Voluntary procedures If the Secretary determines, based on information gathered through the reportable food registry under section 350f of this title or through any other means, that there is a reasonable probability that an article of food (other than infant formula) is adulterated under section 342 of this title or misbranded under section 343(w) of this title and the use of or exposure to such article will cause serious adverse health consequences or death to humans or animals, the Secretary shall provide the responsible party (as defined in section 350f of this title) with an opportunity to cease distribution and recall such article. (b) Prehearing order to cease distribution and give notice (1) In general If the responsible party refuses to or does not voluntarily cease distribution or recall such article within the time and in the manner prescribed by the Secretary (if so prescribed), the Secretary may, by order require, as the Secretary deems necessary, such person to— (A) immediately cease distribution of such article; and (B) as applicable, immediately notify all persons— (i) manufacturing, processing, packing, transporting, distributing, receiving, holding, or importing and selling such article; and (ii) to which such article has been distributed, transported, or sold, to immediately cease distribution of such article. 1 (2) Required additional information (A) In general If an article of food covered by a recall order issued under paragraph (1)(B) has been distributed to a warehouse-based third party logistics provider without providing such provider sufficient information to know or reasonably determine the precise identity of the article of food covered by a recall order that is in its possession, the notice provided by the responsible party subject to the order issued under paragraph (1)(B) shall include such information as is necessary for the warehouse-based third party logistics provider to identify the food. (B) R

Mandatory recall authority (a) Voluntary procedures If the Secretary determines, based on information gathered through the reportable food registry under section 350f of this title or through any other means, that there is a reasonable probability that an article of food (other than infant formula) is adulterated under section 342 of this title or misbranded under section 343(w) of this title and the use of or exposure to such article will cause serious adverse health consequences or death to humans or animals, the Secretary shall provide the responsible party (as defined in section 350f of this title ) with an opportunity to cease distribution and recall such article. (b) Prehearing order to cease distribution and give notice (1) In general If the responsible party refuses to or does not voluntarily cease distribution or recall such article within the time and in the manner prescribed by the Secretary (if so prescribed), the Secretary may, by order require, as the Secretary deems necessary, such person to- (A) immediately cease distribution of such article; and (B) as applicable, immediately notify all persons- (i) manufacturing, processing, packing, transporting, distributing, receiving, holding, or importing and selling such article; and (ii) to which such article has been distributed, transported, or sold, to immediately cease distribution of such article. 1 (2) Required additional information (A) In general If an article of food covered by a recall order issued under paragraph (1)(B) has been distributed to a warehouse-based third party logistics provider without providing such provider sufficient information to know or reasonably determine the precise identity of the article of food covered by a recall order that is in its possession, the notice provided by the responsible party subject to the order issued under paragraph (1)(B) shall include such information as is necessary for the warehouse-based third party logistics provider to identify the food. (B)
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts 21 U.S. Code § 350l, which empowers the Secretary to ensure recalls are appropriately initiated, by granting the supplier 'final authority' on whether a recall is necessary. This shift undermines the manufacturer's (PNC) ability to independently execute a recall deemed necessary by regulators, as noted in the law explanation. The law snippet states "If the Secretary determines...that there is a reasonable probability that an article of food...is adulterated...the Secretary shall provide the responsible party...with an opportunity to cease distribution and recall such article," implying the FDA retains ultimate authority, not the supplier. The changed text infringes upon that authority, making the contract non-compliant.

perturbation.original_text: Each Party will defend and hold harmless the other Party and its subsidiaries, affiliates, officers, directors, employees, attorneys, insurers, shareholders, representatives and agents from and against any and all liabilities, losses, damages, claims, actions, proceedings, suits, costs or expenses, including reasonable attorney fees for counsel retained by the indemnified Party, brought by a Third Party, arising out of or in connection with: 7.1.1 any negligent or intentional act or omission of the indemnifying Party, its agents or employees; 7.1.2 any breach in or default by the indemnifying Party of its obligations under this Agreement; 7.1.3 any other loss, damage or injury caused by or arising out of the indemnifying Party's or its agents' or employees' on-site visits to the indemnified Party's premises; or any claims relating directly to trademark, patent or copyright infringement arising out of a Party's use of the other Party's (or its licensors') trademarks, patents or copyrights as permitted hereunder.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party will defend and hold harmless the other Party and its subsidiaries, affiliates, officers, directors, employees, attorneys, insurers, shareholders, representatives and agents from and against any and all liabilities, losses, damages, claims, actions, proceedings, suits, costs or expenses, including attorney fees, brought by a Third Party, arising out of or in connection with: 7.1.1 any act or omission of the indemnifying Party, its agents or employees; 7.1.2 any default by the indemnifying Party of its obligations under this Agreement; 7.1.3 any other loss, damage or injury caused by or arising out of the indemnifying Party's or its agents' or employees' on-site visits to the indemnified Party's premises; or any claims relating directly to trademark, patent or copyright infringement arising out of a Party's use of the other Party's (or its licensors') trademarks, patents or copyrights as permitted hereunder.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the words 'negligent or intentional' from acts and omissions, and removing the word 'breach' from default, the indemnification clause is broadened beyond what is typically considered reasonable and may violate public policy against indemnifying parties for all actions. This creates uncertainty about the enforceability of this clause.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 2778 establishes rules for interpreting indemnity agreements. By removing the "negligent or intentional" restriction, this section could be interpreted to require indemnity even for actions that were neither negligent nor intentional. This interpretation contradicts the usual understanding of reasonable indemnity, potentially violating legal norms and principles as the agreement requires indemnity beyond what the California Civil Code anticipates.

OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 4. OBLIGATIONS ARISING FROM PARTICULAR TRANSACTIONS [1738 - 3273.69] ( Part 4 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 12. INDEMNITY [2772 - 2784.5] ( Title 12 enacted 1872. ) 2778. In the interpretation of a contract of indemnity, the following rules are to be applied, unless a contrary intention appears: 1. Upon an indemnity against liability, expressly, or in other equivalent terms, the person indemnified is entitled to recover upon becoming liable; 2. Upon an indemnity against claims, or demands, or damages, or costs, expressly, or in other equivalent terms, the person indemnified is not entitled to recover without payment thereof; 3. An indemnity against claims, or demands, or liability, expressly, or in other equivalent terms, embraces the costs of defense against such claims, demands, or liability incurred in good faith, and in the exercise of a reasonable discretion; 4. The person indemnifying is bound, on request of the person indemnified, to defend actions or proceedings brought against the latter in respect to the matters embraced by the indemnity, but the person indemnified has the right to conduct such defenses, if he chooses to do so; 5. If, after reque
perturbation.justification: The changed text broadens the scope of indemnification by removing the "negligent or intentional" restriction from acts and omissions and "breach" from default. This could lead to a requirement to indemnify parties even for actions that were not negligent or intentional, contradicting the typical understanding of reasonable indemnity. California Civil Code § 2778 provides rules for interpreting indemnity agreements. While snippet 1 states that these rules apply "unless a contrary intention appears," the perturbation explanation suggests that removing 'negligent or intentional' could extend indemnity beyond what is legally reasonable or anticipated, potentially violating legal norms.

perturbation.original_text: Such Master Purchase Commitments and any Purchase Orders issued against such Commitments shall be subject exclusively to the terms and conditions of this Agreement. In the event the terms of any Master Purchase Commitment conflicts with the terms of this Agreement, the terms of the Master Purchase Commitment shall control.
perturbation.changed_text: Such Master Purchase Commitments and any Purchase Orders issued against such Commitments shall be subject to the terms and conditions of this Agreement. In the event the terms of any Master Purchase Commitment conflicts with the terms of this Agreement, the Buyer shall determine which terms control.
perturbation.explanation: By stating that 'the Buyer shall determine which terms control,' this introduces ambiguity and grants unilateral power to the buyer in deciding which terms govern, potentially contradicting principles of contract law that emphasize mutual agreement and fair dealing. This could create enforceability issues, especially if the Buyer consistently chooses terms favorable to itself, even if they are less reasonable or customary.
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC § 2-207 addresses the 'battle of the forms' and how conflicting terms in offer and acceptance are handled in sales contracts. By granting the Buyer the power to unilaterally decide which terms control, this circumvents the default rules established by the UCC for determining which terms become part of the contract. This directly contradicts the UCC's goal of establishing a fair and predictable process for resolving conflicting terms in commercial agreements, as it gives one party unchecked power to dictate terms.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-207. Additional Terms in Acceptance or Confirmation. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-207. Additional Terms in Acceptance or Confirmation. (1) A definite and seasonable expression of acceptance or a written confirmation which is sent within a reasonable time operates as an acceptance even though it states terms additional to or different from those offered or agreed upon, unless acceptance is expressly made conditional on assent to the additional or different terms. (2) The additional terms are to be construed as proposals for addition to the contract . Between merchants such terms become part of the contract unless: (a) the offer expressly limits acceptance to the terms of the offer; (b) they materially alter it; or (c) notification of objection to them has already been given or is given within a reasonable time after notice of them is received. (3) Conduct by both parties which recognizes the existence of a contract is sufficient to establish a contract for sale although the writings of the parties do not otherwise establish a contract. In such case the terms of the particular contract consist of those terms on which the writings of the parties agree, together with any supplementary terms incorporated under any other provisions of this Act. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The modification to the contract, giving the Buyer unilateral control over conflicting terms, contradicts the UCC's framework for resolving such conflicts. UCC § 2-207, as quoted in Scraped Law Snippet 1, addresses how additional terms in acceptance or confirmation are handled. By allowing the Buyer to decide which terms control, the changed text circumvents the UCC's aim of establishing a fair and predictable process. This undermines the UCC's provisions designed to determine which terms become part of the contract, and gives undue power to one party, potentially leading to unfair outcomes. This falls under the definition of 'Misaligned Terminology Legal Contradiction' because it deviates from legally recognized definitions making the contract unenforceable.
